q: K-th largest in unsorted N-element array?
a: QuickSelect O(N) avg case, OR min-heap of size K — O(N log K). Beats full sort.

q: Top-K from a stream — data structure?
a: Min-heap of size K. Each new item: if larger than min, pop min and push new. O(log K) per item, O(K) memory.
Two heaps: max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper. Rebalance after insert. Median = root(s).
DFS or BFS from each unvisited node. Each start = one new component. O(V+E).
DFS with "currently on stack" set. Encountering a node on the stack = back-edge = cycle. Or Kahn\'s topo sort with count check.
Min-heap of K head pointers. Pop smallest, push its next. O(N log K) — strictly better than pairwise merge or full sort for small K.
Monotone invariant: adding to window can\'t un-break the property once broken. Without monotonicity, sliding window may not apply.
O(N) time, O(1) extra space on sorted arrays. Each pointer moves forward at most N times. Strictly better than hash-set on space.
When answer space is monotonic: predicate(x) — small fails, big works. Boundary searchable in O(log N) evaluations of predicate.

q: Prefix sums — what they buy you?
a: Range-sum queries in O(1) after O(N) precompute. Difference: prefix[r]-prefix[l]. Use also for "subarray sum equals K."
Clears the lowest set bit. Repeat to count bits. Used in Brian Kernighan\'s bit count.
Array has every value twice except one. XOR all values: pairs cancel; singleton remains. O(N) time, O(1) memory.

q: Dijkstra fails on negative edges because?
a: Locks in a node\'s distance once extracted from PQ. Negative edges can later offer a shorter path. Use Bellman-Ford instead.
O(VE). Handles negative edges. Detects negative cycles (one extra relaxation pass — if any edge can still relax, cycle exists).
"Next greater/smaller element," "stock span," "trapping rain water." Each element pushed once + popped once = O(N).
A* = Dijkstra + heuristic h(n). With admissible heuristic (never overestimates), A* finds optimal + prunes search dramatically when target known.
Pure push: celebs cause write amplification. Pure pull: high-followers users slow reads. Hybrid: push for normal, pull for celebs above threshold.
During a PARTITION. With healthy network, you can have both. Pacific PACELC adds the steady-state latency-vs-consistency dimension.
PA/EL — availability + low latency (default; tunable per-query). Spanner is PC/EC — consistency always.

q: Consistent hashing add N+1th node — what fraction remaps?
a: Roughly 1/(N+1) of keys. With virtual nodes, those keys redistribute evenly across the existing nodes.
Effectively impossible in distributed systems. Settle for "at-least-once + idempotent receivers" or "at-most-once + accept loss."
Singleflight (only one request rebuilds, others wait) OR refresh-ahead (rebuild before TTL expires, serve stale during).
Replicate hot keys across multiple nodes + load-balance reads. Consistent hashing doesn\'t help when ONE key is hot.
Redis with Lua script — atomic + low-latency. In-memory per-server can\'t enforce a global cap.

q: Token bucket vs sliding-window for rate limits?
a: Token bucket: tolerates bursts up to bucket size. Sliding-window counter: cheap + approximate. Pick by burst tolerance.
Dedupe table TTL longer than retry window (~30 days typical). Shorter risks duplicates that arrive late.

q: For chat ordering with simultaneous sends?
a: Server-assigned per-conversation sequence number. Wall-clock skews. Sequence is monotonic + deterministic.

q: Range vs hash sharding — range when?
a: When you need range queries (e.g., "rows where date BETWEEN X and Y"). Risk: hot partitions. Hash distributes evenly but kills ranges.
Session pinning: route user reads to primary for N seconds after their write. Gives RYW consistency without sacrificing replica scaling for others.
Each step has compensating action. On failure, run compensations in reverse. Standard when 2PC is impractical.
Prevent cascading failures. After N failures, open breaker → fast-fail subsequent calls → probe for recovery. Don\'t overload the downstream.
Append-only event log. Current state derived by replaying events. Snapshots + projections are derived views for fast reads.
SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY group ORDER BY date). GROUP BY collapses; you\'d lose per-row detail.
ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY group ORDER BY metric DESC) → filter rn ≤ N in an outer query.
LAG(col, n): previous nth row\'s value. LEAD: next. Useful for diffs, deltas, conversion funnels.

q: Composite index on (a, b, c) — usable for WHERE b=X?
a: NO. Leftmost-prefix rule: must use leftmost column (a). WHERE a=X, (a, b), (a, b, c) all work; skipping a doesn\'t.
Index that contains all columns needed by query (key + INCLUDE columns). DB satisfies query from index alone; no table fetch.
UPDATE/DELETE leave dead tuples (multi-version concurrency). VACUUM reclaims dead-tuple space + ANALYZE updates planner statistics.
OLTP: row-oriented, single-record write/read, normalized (Postgres, MySQL). OLAP: columnar, batch aggregates, denormalized (Snowflake, ClickHouse).
Star: fact + denormalized dim tables. Fewer joins, faster queries. Snowflake: normalized dims (more joins). Star = analytical default.
DELETE WHERE partition=X then INSERT. Task is replayable; re-runs don\'t duplicate. Standard pipeline pattern.

q: For analytics on a 1TB Postgres table?
a: Move analytics workload to columnar OLAP (Snowflake / ClickHouse / DuckDB). Postgres is bad at aggregate scans on big tables.
Bronze (raw) → Silver (cleaned/normalized) → Gold (business-ready aggregates). Standard layer model for Delta Lake / Iceberg.
Avro/Protobuf schemas tracked centrally with backward/forward compat rules. Lets producers + consumers evolve independently.
Three open table formats over Parquet. Provide ACID + time travel + schema evolution on data lakes. Iceberg is most vendor-neutral; Delta is Databricks-leaning.
"My weakness is I work too hard" / "I don\'t really fail." Fake-failure dodges are instant rejects.
Pull-not-push: what attracts you forward; light positive on current employer. Never badmouth — signals you\'ll do same about them later.
